codeReview.summary: The implementation extracted content from the wrong lecture PDFs. Feature description expected Lecture 9 to cover 'modular programming, recursion, BIOS/DOS interrupts, TSRs' and Lecture 10 to cover 'comprehensive review, real-world examples, exam preparation'. Instead, the implementation extracted Lecture 9 as 'Memory Hierarchy (cont)' and Lecture 10 as 'Exceptional Control Flow' - content that appears to be from a Computer Systems/Architecture course (CS:APP style) rather than Assembly Language Programming.

issues.description: Lecture 9 content mismatch: Feature description says 'modular programming, recursion, BIOS/DOS interrupts, TSRs' but implementation extracted 'Memory Hierarchy (cont)' with topics like Locality, Caching, Direct-mapped/Set Associative/Fully Associative caches. This is Computer Systems content, not Assembly Language content.

Lecture 10 content mismatch: Feature description says 'comprehensive review, real-world examples, exam preparation' but implementation extracted 'Exceptional Control Flow' with topics like Exceptions, Processes, Context Switching, Virtual Memory, Page Tables, TLB. This is Operating Systems/Computer Architecture content, not Assembly Language exam preparation.

Lecture 9 concepts array contains 'locality', 'spatial-locality', 'temporal-locality', 'memory-hierarchy', 'caching', 'cache-hit', 'cache-miss' - none of these are assembly language concepts per se. BIOS/DOS interrupts (INT instruction) are assembly-relevant but not present.

Lecture 10 instructions array contains only 'INT' and 'IRET' - but the lecture is about Virtual Memory, Page Tables, TLB which are architecture concepts, not assembly instructions. The INT instruction context (DOS interrupts for I/O) should be present per feature description but is missing.

Lecture 9 provenance shows 'Lecture 9.pdf' with 81 pages and extractionConfidence: 0.85. If the correct PDF was used but the wrong content was extracted, the source file reference may be correct but the content extraction was wrong.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md specifies 'Every content object must preserve provenance metadata' and 'Do not invent course facts and present them as source truth.' The extracted content violates this by presenting Computer Systems content as if it came from an Assembly Language course. The provenance metadata is preserved but the content itself may not match the actual source material.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: lectures.json:1439 - lecture 9 id=9 has title 'Memory Hierarchy (cont)' with provenance sourceFile='Lecture 9.pdf', but feature description expected modular programming/BIOS/DOS/TSRs

sharedStateObservations.observation: The validation contract (validation-contract.md) may not have specific assertions to verify that extracted content matches the feature description expectations. Content validation ran 611 assertions but only validated format and structure, not semantic correctness relative to feature requirements.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff shows 'npm run validate:content' with exitCode 0 and '611 content assertions passed' but this doesn't catch the semantic mismatch between feature description and actual content

sharedStateObservations.observation: The feature's skillName is 'frontend-worker' but the work was content extraction (data processing). No skill feedback was provided in the transcript skeleton to indicate this mismatch or any awareness that the wrong content was being extracted.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Feature skillName='frontend-worker' but work involved PDF parsing and JSON generation in scripts/build-instruction-index.ts pattern

summary: FAIL - The implementation extracted content from the wrong source material. Lecture 9 was expected to cover modular programming, recursion, BIOS/DOS interrupts, and TSRs (Assembly Language topics), but instead extracted Memory Hierarchy and Caching content (Computer Systems topics). Lecture 10 was expected to cover comprehensive review and exam preparation, but instead extracted Exceptional Control Flow, Virtual Memory, and Page Tables content. This suggests either: (1) wrong PDFs were processed, or (2) correct PDFs were used but the wrong lectures within those PDFs were identified. The course is ACS2906 Assembly Language Programming - the extracted content appears to be from a computer architecture/systems course (likely CS:APP based on Memory Hierarchy and Exceptional Control Flow topics). All technical aspects (JSON structure, provenance metadata, validation) are correct, but the semantic content does not match the feature description or course context.
